proposalTitle: Adversarial Intimacy Exploitation (RSD Larry Willis)
proposalAbstract: The development of observational acuity for anomalous or suspicious behavior detection requires a deep familiarity akin to intimacy within the adversarial recruitment, training and deployment attack continuum best acquired through experienced practitioners directly involved in persistent observation and interaction coupled with field research.  This provides a baseline understanding of the common variables across threat vectors to include the identification of behavior associated with, but not limited to criminal and terrorist threats against critical infrastructure, active shooter and suicide bombers.  Developing, validating, delivering and measuring training related to enhanced preparedness, situational awareness and interdiction has wide application within the homeland security enterprise.
topicTitle: Modeling, Simulation, and Gaming Technologies

proposalTitle: Standoff Explosives Trace Detector Test and Evaluation
proposalAbstract:
The Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Laboratory (JHU/APL), a division of the Johns Hopkins University and a non-profit University Affiliated Research Center (UARC), proposes a series of test and evaluation (T&E) activities over a 21-month period to develop a standoff explosives detection capability. The primary task proposed is T&E for a quantum-cascade laser-based, infrared reflectance detector prototype device from EOS Photonics.  JHU/APL proposes to perform this task in two test phases:  characterization and performance. Test and evaluation of the EOS Photonics device will include detection and operational suitability based upon input from the DHS Customer.  
The effort also includes four optional tasks. One task consists of additional testing of the EOS Photonics prototype if vendor updates stemming from the initial test results are made. Another optional task includes testing of standoff explosives trace detection devices from other vendors. The third optional task involves the development of an augmented sample preparation capability. This task, which would include method development and validation, ensures that samples for the T&E are prepared in the most rigorous and defensible manner that can currently be achieved.  The final optional task involves surveying the current state-of-the-art in image processing algorithms, developing an RFI solicitation, and JHU/APL competitively selecting an academia partner to collaborate with on a feasibility demonstration of a promising concept in the areas of compressive sensing and coded aperture processing.
topicTitle: Trace Detection of Explosives
companyName: The Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Laboratory

proposalTitle: BIOSwimmer: In-Liquid Inspection System
proposalAbstract: This project addresses a critical need in the nation`s infrastructure and border protection, inspection inside tanks on large ships in port. These missions must be performed in a timely, effective, and safe manner in challenging environments. The BIOSwimmer system is an end-to-end solution that includes sensing, post processing, control, and a biomimetic autonomous swimming vehicle that provides efficiency, maneuverability and payload integration capability largely unavailable in other platforms. This PH II includes development of advanced sensing to perform in-liquid inspections with accuracy and reliability as well as design and prototyping in support of a Phase II vehicle build and demonstration. Additional work includes developing support equipment and testing them in realistic situations. The team will work hand in hand with end users, DHS, and actual tanker owners to provide an appropriate solution. A full specification effort is followed by the creation of a system specification for review with end users. Testing efforts provide confidence in the resulting product`s reliability, safety and ability to be certified and approved for use in the field. PH II provides a feasible, cost effective solution with a direct path towards commercialization and insertion into several relevant markets. Commercialization is a major focus of PH II.
topicTitle: Autonomous (or alternative) In-Liquid Survey Vehicle

proposalTitle: Integrating Canine Behavior and Analytical Chemistry to Support Enhanced Canine Generalization and the development of Non-Detonable Training Tools for Explosives Detection
proposalAbstract: Explosive detection canines (EDCs) are an integral component of the Homeland Security Enterprise; however, little information is known regarding EDC psychological perception of explosive materials, leaving substantial gaps in our understanding of canine capabilities and how to optimize training and performance. This project addresses three primary knowledge gaps to establish fundamental knowledge in canine explosives detection. Objective 1 will determine canine detection thresholds for a range of explosive-associated compounds in parallel to analytical analysis of the headspace of explosives. Using this information, we will establish the first canine perception database, providing headspace concentration and canine detection limits for a range of explosive associated compounds. This will allow us to estimate odor value units, or the estimated relative contribution of headspace components to canine perception. Objective 2 will leverage this information to create odor simulants that can be used for in situ operational assessments. We will prepare and robustly test a range of simulants for spontaneous generalization from live materials, allowing for safe assessment of already operational EDCs in the field. Objective 3 will use multiple exemplar training (concept formation training) to re-imagine how non-detonable training aids can be used to train robust detection and generalization to a range of target explosives. Using a series of overlapping but variable training aids designed to produce detection of a specifically targeted explosive signature, we will evaluate generalization and determine the number of exemplars (or training aids) that lead to robust generalization across a category of explosives. Successful results in the laboratory will be generalized to operational teams in Phase II experiments.
topicTitle: Detection Canine Technologies

proposalTitle: Non-contact Surface and Vapor Sampler for Trace Explosive Screening
proposalAbstract: The primary objective of this LRBAA is to develop enhanced contact and non-contact trace explosives sampling methods and prototypes that are seamlessly integrated with or integration-ready with currently deployed Explosives Trace Detectors (ETDs) and/or Next Generation ETDs. The non-contact sampling methods and prototypes are developed for use in cargo, checked baggage, and or checkpoint screening in aviation security environments.
topicTitle: Enhanced Contact and Non-Contact Trace Explosives Sampling and Detection
awardProposalAbstract: The primary objective of this LRBAA is to develop enhanced contact and non-contact trace explosives sampling methods and prototypes that are seamlessly integrated with or integration-ready with currently deployed Explosives Trace Detectors (ETDs) and/or Next Generation ETDs. The non-contact sampling methods and prototypes are developed for use in cargo, checked baggage, and or checkpoint screening in aviation security environments.

proposalTitle: Mobile EDS CHeckpoint
proposalAbstract: Computed Tomography (CT) Explosive Detection Systems (EDS), like the IDSS DETECTTM 1000, have demonstrated significant improvements in Automated Threat Detection (ATD) and image quality for On-Screen Resolution (OSR) of prohibited items compared to projection X-ray security scanners.  TSA and other DHS components, such as USSS, CBP as well as DoD have expressed the need for a Mobile EDS solution to provide capability to establish a temporary checkpoint security scanner at various locations and venues with short notice.  IDSS is proposing the development of a Checkpoint CT Scanner integrated into a mobile platform to address the needs of USSS and others.
topicTitle: Explosive Detection in Checked Baggage- Development and Maturation of System Components and Subsystems
awardProposalAbstract: Computed Tomography (CT) Explosive Detection Systems (EDS), like the IDSS DETECTTM 1000, have demonstrated significant improvements in Automated Threat Detection (ATD) and image quality for On-Screen Resolution (OSR) of prohibited items compared to projection X-ray security scanners.  TSA and other DHS components, such as USSS, CBP as well as DoD have expressed the need for a Mobile EDS solution to provide capability to establish a temporary checkpoint security scanner at various locations and venues with short notice.  IDSS is proposing the development of a Checkpoint CT Scanner integrated into a mobile platform to address the needs of USSS and others.

proposalTitle: Advancing Trusted Information Sharing, Safeguarding, and Federated ICAM in the Public Safety Community Through the Maturation of Trustmark Framework Software Capabilities
proposalAbstract: Information Sharing and Safeguarding (IS&S) is a fundamental need of the U.S. public safety (PS) community. The community's IS&S requirements are substantial and increasing, as it must respond to a wide range of challenging, multi-agency emergencies. Federated Identity, Credential, and Access Management (Federated ICAM) is a critical capability that can help to advance PS IS&S capabilities across agency boundaries. Advancement of technical capabilities and solutions in these areas is critical to help the PS community fulfill its mission of ensuring the protection and well-being of U.S. citizens. Since 2005, GTRI has played a prominent technical leadership role in advancing the IS&S and Federated ICAM capabilities of the PS community. One of our most promising contributions to the PS community during this period has been to design, build, and pilot the trustmark framework, which provides standards, artifacts, software tools, and methodologies for managing IS&S and Federated ICAM trust. Despite our work to date on the trustmark framework, it has not yet reached a level of technical maturity that would enable its wide-scale adoption. Perhaps the most pressing maturity gap in the trustmark framework is the lack of mature software tools to support the framework's primary use cases. To help the PS community better fulfill its mission, GTRI proposes a project that will produce substantial advancements in the maturity of key trustmark framework software tools. This project will provide substantial value to the PS community as well as the agencies and divisions within DHS that support the PS community.
topicTitle: Identity Management

proposalTitle: Extremist Violent and Nonviolent United States (EVAN-US) Database
proposalAbstract: The overarching goal of the EVAN-US Database project is to create a yearly dataset that tracks the organizational, behavioral, and networking characteristics of a key sample of United States hate and extremist organizations both violent and non-violent in nature.  The research team will create a database that is yearly and has a key set of organizational variables. These variables can be used to identify the organizations most likely to perpetrate violence and the method of attack.  The landscape of domestic violent extremism in the United States is changing both in terms of ideology and organizational dynamics. This effort will help policy makers get a better handle on where new threats are coming from- and the likelihood of organizations deploying violence.  Analysis of the project data will provide unique insights into the variables that facilitate both non-violent indoctrination and the transition to violent extremism. Once identified, those variables will become the basis of a rapid assessment tool that enables policy makers and law enforcement to gauge the risk for violent extremism from homegrown organizations.
topicTitle: Targeted Violence Prevention
awardProposalAbstract: The overarching goal of the EVAN-US Database project is to create a yearly dataset that tracks the organizational, behavioral, and networking characteristics of a key sample of United States hate and extremist organizations both violent and non-violent in nature.  The research team will create a database that is yearly and has a key set of organizational variables. These variables can be used to identify the organizations most likely to perpetrate violence and the method of attack.  The landscape of domestic violent extremism in the United States is changing both in terms of ideology and organizational dynamics. This effort will help policy makers get a better handle on where new threats are coming from- and the likelihood of organizations deploying violence.  Analysis of the project data will provide unique insights into the variables that facilitate both non-violent indoctrination and the transition to violent extremism. Once identified, those variables will become the basis of a rapid assessment tool that enables policy makers and law enforcement to gauge the risk for violent extremism from homegrown organizations.

proposalTitle: IMSAR Counter Unmanned Aerial Systems Solution: New Use for a Proven Technology
proposalAbstract:
Small Unmanned Aerial Systems (sUAS) are increasingly becoming threats to public safety and homeland security. Current C-UAS solutions include ground-based optical and/or radar sensors that have LOS and maneuverability limitations or lack the ability to observe sUAS at relevant distances. IMSAR’s proposed solution provides a low-Size, Weight, Power, and Cost (SWaP-C) airborne radar sensor with enhanced C-UAS capability that is based on our existing mature tactical Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance (ISR) airborne radar products. The proposed multimode airborne active radar system will enable DHS to perform C-UAS missions from manned or unmanned, fixed or rotary-wing, or tethered platforms by directly detecting the presence of airborne threats, day or night, in adverse weather, and even in the absence of the target sUAS communications or control signals.

DHS’s airborne assets equipped with this technology will be able to automatically search, track, and identify sUAS threats in varying environments, achieving a mobile wide-area, bird’s-eye view of airborne threats. During a typical Concept of Operations (CONOP) of an airborne radar-based C-UAS system, the airborne DHS platform will orbit a site or fly a perimeter and scan sectors to identify all moving airborne objects. Based on sophisticated algorithms IMSAR will develop in this effort, the proposed C-UAS system will be able to detect, locate, and track these airborne moving objects, as well as discriminate between sUAS and other objects, such as birds. In addition to tracking the sUAS, the proposed system will be able to calculate a Predicted Intercept Point (PIP) to anticipate the future movements of the sUAS targets and to cue a mitigation or neutralization device, giving DHS an additional tool to help protect sensitive locations and infrastructure, borders, or events and large gatherings.

The overall objectives of this proposal are to provide airborne radar-based C-UAS capability that scales to the different airborne platforms used by DHS and achieves the following:
Extend the range at which threat sUAS can be detected, tracked, identified, and mitigated
Expand the terrain and environmental conditions in which DHS can effectively perform C-UAS missions
Detect, track, and classify dozens of targets simultaneously
Increase the Probability of Detection (Pd) and Probability of Correct Classification (PCC) of airborne targets without relying on sUAS communications or control signals

Once the technology has been successfully developed into a TRL 8 capability, it will become part of IMSAR’s core capabilities that will scale through all of our products. Transition partners could include any current and future consumers of IMSAR low-SWaP radar systems. These potential partners include several Department of Defense components as well as county sheriff’s offices and fire protection services.

proposalTitle: Study of the DETECT 1000 Dual Energy CT performance against Liquid Aerosols and Gels (LAGs) and Thin Targets
proposalAbstract: Demonstrate the ability of a  Checkpoint Explosive Detection System to properly isolate Thin Sheet Explosives in laptops and small electronics as well as alarm on liquid threats and clear safe liquids in common carry-on baggage.
topicTitle: Risk-Based Screening

proposalTitle: Accurate Illicit Drug Detector for Unopened Packages
proposalAbstract:
One Resonance LLC will deliver a ruggedize, unprecedently accurate, non-intrusive package inspection scanner to thwart the illicit drugs supply chain and protect mail inspection personnel from exposure to dangerous chemicals. 
The core detection technology, Quadrupole Resonance (QR), provides chemical-specific identification of illicit substances in packages, without the need for image interpretation. QR has been demonstrated to be an effective method for the detection of explosives and illicit drugs such as methamphetamine. One Resonance solution presents several advantages over alternative available inspection technologies. QR detection performance does not depend on how the drug is distributed or camouflaged inside a package. The QR sensor detects the total drug material, independently of its shape. QRs chemical specificity can identify and differentiate illicit substances from benign materials with a remarkably low false-positive rate.
Furthermore, One Resonance has proven to have an effective process to adapt the scanner to detect new illicit substances of interest. The goals for this project are: (i) to demonstrate that the QR technology can effectively detect a new list of illegal drugs; (ii) validate the detection performance and refine the CONOPS at one of the five Postal Service International Service Centers. In addition to supporting CBP and USPS needs for international and national mail inspection, One Resonance will reach out to private carriers to assess the CONOPS and gauge the interest in the new screening modality.  Through this project, One Resonance will leverage the successful results and positive feedback received during the 2019 DHS Opioid Detection Challenge.

proposalTitle: Update to CT-Based Break-Bulk and Pallet Cargo Screening System Leveraging Existing CTDE Qualified Checkpoint Baggage Cost-Effective Technology
proposalAbstract: IDSS has addressed the comments provided by DHS S&T and now established a more cost effective and better defined solution for cargo pallet screening that can meet the objectives defined by DHS S&T and TSA. This design utilizes a unique approach to scanning large pallets by incorporating a horizontally mounted CT gantry, which is raised to move a pallet into position and then is lowered to quickly scan a pallet from the bottom to the 65-inch height. By utilizing a horizontally mounted CT gantry, the CT rotational subsystem and pallet movement is simplified while cost of manufacturing the unit is significantly reduced. IDSS has a target price for the final system of $425,000, which provides a very attractive solution for Cargo shippers as a cost effective and efficient way to address TSA screening requirements.
topicTitle: Air Cargo Security

proposalTitle: Leveraging Geospatial Intelligence to Improve Community Resilience
proposalAbstract: The Geospatial Community Resilience Workshop project will bring together GEOINT and First Responder subject matter experts to share knowledge and develop strategies to better utilize ubiquitous geospatial information in order to improve community resilience.  As a unifying construct, geospatial data underpins planning and mitigation efforts in a way that will allow first responders and emergency managers to design more resilient communities and improve emergency response prior to, and after, manmade or natural disasters or critical infrastructure loss.  Workshop participants will develop multiple strategic pathways to better leverage GEOINT against those critical resilience requirements using flooding as a foundation model.  Derived pathways will be captured in a written knowledge product articulating recommendations to the Department of Homeland Security for inclusion in the National Flood Decision Support Toolbox (NFDST).  These strategies will be transportable to all forms of disaster mitigation and improving resilience.
topicTitle: Community Resilience

proposalTitle: First Responder Community and FirstNet: Enabling Practical, Resilient Situational Awareness and Decision Making
proposalAbstract:
Created by Congress in 2012, the FirstNet Program mission is to establish, operate, and maintain an interoperable public safety broadband network dedicated to public safety, emergency management and the First Responder Community. Fundamental to the FirstNet mission is collaboration among state, local, tribal and Federal resources for effective incident response. It is estimated the FirstNet public safety network, when operational, will include more than 5 million people, vehicles, electronic devices, and other pieces of gear.

To realize interoperability and mission success, FirstNet requires a strategy to advance practical and resilient capabilities that facilitate incident situational awareness and operational decision making. Furthermore, the strategy must facilitate analyses and timely, secure sharing of appropriately synthesized information for incident responders. To accomplish this strategy, FirstNet is dependent upon digital identity and attribute standards, capabilities and technologies that facilitate synchronized registration, verification, and authorization of FirstNet users, devices, and gear across the various communities. This must be paired with efficient information tagging capabilities and digital authorization policies that accelerate data analysis and synthesis for operational decision making. This strategy should also advance policies, standards and technologies that facilitate a tangible, ubiquitous identity management, data content optimization and distribution framework while optimizing usability for the First Responder, FirstNet, and other emergency management communities. For these purposes, our proposal recommends establishing a framework, resources, and capable expertise led and coordinated by DHS Science and Technology.
topicTitle: Trend and Pattern Identification

proposalTitle: DETECT 320CS Adaptive Screening
proposalAbstract: A CT skid scanner that can adapt to occasional regions of high density within the skid would provide more operational value to user’s screening CONOPS and decrease the need to procure multiple machines to scan different types of cargo. By modifying the operation of the 320CS within the constraints of the current hardware, the proposed work offers a low-risk way to extend this improved security screening capability to a wider range of cargo. IDSS will implement enhancements to the 320CS operation to expand its capability for dense materials. In addition, IDSS has developed a concept for imaging drums of liquid submitted for carriage on aircraft, which based on IDSS’s participation in the TSA’s Air Cargo Security Technology Working Group’s (Air Cargo STWG) Air Cargo Security Technology Capability Gap Process is a priority market need. These enhancements would use the same hardware as the current 320CS but increase penetration by trading off throughput against penetration of higher density materials. The 320CS would adaptively adjust the scanning time based on density information automatically collected from the cargo being scanned.

proposalTitle: E1 HEMP Vulnerability Assessment of 5G Cellular Systems
proposalAbstract:
The primary goal of this project is to evaluate the vulnerability of 5G cellular systems when exposed to early-time (E1) high-altitude electromagnetic pulse (HEMP) transient electromagnetic fields. A HEMP event is created when a nuclear weapon is detonated at high altitude above earth. The E1 portion of the HEMP event is characterized as an electromagnetic plane wave of very high electric field amplitude (tens of kV/m) and extremely short duration (100s of nanoseconds). This electromagnetic energy propagates toward the earth’s surface and can couple to exposed conductors, generating voltage and current transients, or directly irradiate equipment, potentially causing equipment damage or disruption. E1 HEMP can affect extremely large geographic areas, representing the ground area contained between the detonation location and the horizon in all directions. While not all affected areas receive the same level of HEMP energy, a single nuclear weapon detonation could potentially impact sensitive equipment across much of the continental U.S. This project also proposes validation of cable modelling methods developed by University of Illinois Critical Infrastructure Resilience Institute (CIRI) through testing. 

To support an existing portfolio of E1 HEMP-related research projects, EPRI has developed extensive laboratory capabilities including North America’s largest commercial RS105 guided wave test facility located in Charlotte, N.C. This laboratory enables EPRI researchers to expose equipment and systems to threat level, HEMP-like transient electromagnetic fields to perform electrical measurements and determine equipment susceptibility levels. EPRI will place the cable structure inside the RS105 facility test volume and expose the cable system to HEMP-like transient electromagnetic fields to cause coupling to the cable’s conductors. 

The primary project goal will be achieved by obtaining cellular tower equipment donations from various industry partners. These systems will be configured to function as operational 5G cellular towers and/or small cells and will be placed inside the RS105 test volume. While operational, the equipment will be exposed to gradually increasing E1 HEMP-like electromagnetic fields, while observing the equipment’s response and state-of-health. This testing will continue until the equipment is either destroyed or the output limit of the test equipment (50 kV/m) is reached. 

To accomplish the model validation tasks, EPRI will collaborate with CIRI to develop a cable structure that can be modelled using CIRI’s methods. Measurements will be performed at predetermined locations to capture the current and/or voltage developed within the cables due to the HEMP coupling. These measurements can then be compared to CIRI’s simulation results to validate CIRI’s methods. Additionally, EPRI will use a vector network analyzer (VNA) to determine the scattering parameters (S-parameters) of the cable at various relevant frequencies. These measurements can be compared to the S-parameters of CIRI’s models for further validation of the modeling approach.

Computer modeling efforts running in parallel with the testing tasks will enable determination of the stresses that maybe imparted on various system components during an E1 HEMP event. By comparing the modeling results (stress) with the test results (strength), a vulnerability assessment can be performed. The results of this vulnerability assessment will then be used to determine and test, if appropriate, mitigation approaches and technologies that may be used to protect 5G cellular systems from E1 HEMP threats. At conclusion of the project, a hardened 5G small cell system will be produced and demonstrated to function within the E1 HEMP environment.

proposalTitle: Millimeter-wave shoe scanner
proposalAbstract:
According to the International Air Transport Association (IATA) among a survey of 142 airports, before September 11, 2001, approximately 350 people passed through security checkpoints every hour. A November 2011 survey found that processing times fell to about 149 passengers per hour. Passengers are currently required to divest outer layers of clothing before they pass through security checkpoints. Clothing divestment, especially shoes, is a significant impediment to efficient screening. Reduced throughput in security checkpoints increases inconvenience to passengers, which ultimately leads to lost revenue for airports and lost productivity for the general economy.

Integrating a millimeter-wave (mm-wave) imaging array into the floor of the current millimeter-wave advanced imaging technology portal would remove the need for passengers to divest shoes before being screened. The unique advantage of this solution is the increased capability without an increase in checkpoint footprint. This solution would also decrease the load on operators scanning objects passing through carry-on luggage x-ray scanners, possibly reducing manpower needed to operate a checkpoint.
topicTitle: Checkpoint Passenger Screening

proposalTitle: Identity Capability Exploration
proposalAbstract: A streamlined Identity Capability Exploration (ICEx) process would enable the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Science and Technology Directorate (S&T) and the Office of Biometric Identity Management (OBIM) Futures Identity to expedite the identification, evaluation, maturation, and operational deployment of new biometric and identity capabilities to support the dynamic mission sets of DHS Component organizations. DHS OBIM will require a streamlined process to rapidly evaluate new technologies, or expand existing technologies such as rapid DNA pilots, to ensure performance, interoperability, and security posture before integration and migration to DHS enterprise systems such as Homeland Advanced Recognition Technology. The Booz Allen ICEx team will provide end-to-end technical, SME, and operational support across 8 task areas: 1) Next Generation Identity Research, 2) Proof-of-Concept Development, 3) Prototype Development, 4) Biometric Standards Development and Support, 5) Concept Prototype Environment, 6) Biometrics and Identity SME Support, 7) Procurement and Integration of Hardware and Software, and 8) Cloud Support. By adopting this repeatable process, we will reduce the effort, costs, and timelines required to research, develop, and deploy new biometric and identity capabilities to DHS OBIM in support of DHS Component missions. Our multi-stage evaluation and maturation approach provides successive evaluations and decision points to ensure additional resources and time are cost-effectively dedicated only to candidate technologies that demonstrate potential for operational deployment and that DHS integration and mission requirements, including required technical attributes and performance expectations, are satisfied before operational deployment.

proposalTitle: National Pilot Program:  EMPOWER
proposalAbstract:
The State University of New York at Albany proposes the development and pilot of EMPOWER, a next-generation scalable decision-support tool suite for the emergency management enterprise. EMPOWER integrates advanced analytics, real-time localized high resolution mesonet-based weather data, critical infrastructure “lifelines” and social vulnerability data, with novel visualization capabilities to provide rapid assessment of changing weather conditions and their potential impacts on communities and critical infrastructure. EMPOWER leverages state and federal computing investments, integrates with existing state-of-the-art weather observational infrastructure and data pipelines, aligns with crisis decision-making principles and protective action repertoires to protect lives and property, and provides modular capabilities for future integration of additional advanced sensor-based weather hazard monitoring networks. In short, EMPOWER equips the emergency management enterprise today with tools, data, and decision-support analytics to manage the weather emergencies of tomorrow. The work in EMPOWER is organized around the following 5 tasks: 
 
Task 1: Connect Federal Emergency Response with NYS Mesonet Data  
 
Task 2: Map current decision-making practices, processes, and systems across jurisdictions and conduct stakeholder dialogue  
 
Task 3: Fusing Weather Hazard and Social, Built-Environment, and Critical Infrastructure Vulnerability Information.  
 
Task 4: Develop Technology-Enhanced, Cross-Professional Simulations for Training and Exercise 
 
Task 5: International Cooperation and Dissemination of Research Findings and Practical Recommendations  
 
The impact of weather-related disasters on communities can be reduced significantly through improved access to higher quality and more relevant hazard and vulnerability data for enhanced situational awareness. EMPOWER will provide whole community emergency managers with more timely, precise, and contextually relevant weather hazard information enabling them to make smarter critical protective action decisions and resource deployments when preparing for and responding to extreme weather events.

proposalTitle: DECIDE 4 Transportation
proposalAbstract: Distributed Environment for Critical Infrastructure Decision-making Exercises, DECIDE, was developed to engage the financial sector leadership and business process owners in situational awareness of cyber security impacts and effects at the sector and business operations level. Under a current DHS contract, NUARI is extending the DECIDE tool to support exercises across the energy sector. DECIDE creates a focused and collaborative environment to explore internal, firm-to-firm, and sector understanding of the systems, processes, regulatory, and legal effects of cyber security events. DECIDE provides a natural coupling of operations with education and training that allows probing a sector and its dependencies to proactively identify, analyze, assess and coordinate activities to mitigate critical infrastructure and systemic risk. DECIDE and associated financial sector exercises have been critical drivers for the development of a Financial Sector Playbook and greater situational awareness of cyber security impacts on tactics, techniques, and procedures. Use of the DECIDE Platform strengthens the organizational capability to respond to and recover from cyber security events, as well as the ability to execute these plans under conditions of operational duress. In highly interconnected infrastructures, response across multiple sectors is essential for survivability. DECIDE allows learning with context at the individual and sector levels. NUARI proposes the development of a multi-sector focused DECIDE platform, which includes engagement with and outreach to firms, industry organizations, and information-sharing entities.

proposalTitle: Explosive Detection Devices for Checkpoint Security
proposalAbstract:
Current screening technologies in use at airports and other security checkpoints are likely to miss explosives that are hidden in electronic devices. One Resonance Sensors, LLC is developing Nuclear Quadrupole Resonance-based portable electronic scanners as a response to this threat. Small amounts of plastic or powder explosives concealed in electronic devices are identified by using a specific sequence of radio-frequency pulses. The scanner uses chemically specific detection protocols to interrogate the interior of electronic devices, providing a simple Clear or Alarm response in less than 10 seconds. The system will be upgradable to add new threats without hardware changes.
Under the proposed project One Resonance Sensors, LLC will: (1) optimize, test and validate the smart phone scanner, (2) develop and test a device that scans both smart phones and tablets and, (3) use the Nuclear Quadrupole Resonance technology to address other checkpoint needs.
The commercial application of the effort is the deployment of screening devices to security checkpoints. This is relevant to the missions of TSA and USSS, but also responds to needs for added security at critical infrastructure facilities and events.
topicTitle: Risk-Based Screening

proposalTitle: Standoff Difference Raman Spectroscopy with Automated Depth Scanning
proposalAbstract:
Explosive threats are a major concern in aviation security and across Homeland Security Enterprise. DHS S&T seeks solutions to enhance current transportation screening capabilities to meet evolving threats, ensuring safety of the traveling public, Transportation Safety Officers, and others within the transportation environment. 

The specific goal of this effort is to develop a technology that can selectively identify explosives through bottles and other containers. The solution proposed allows rapid, safe and confident Alarm Resolution by Transportation Security Officers (TSO) with minimal training, for small bulk threats in containers. It reduces the frequency of Advanced Alarm Resolution, reduces the reliance on Transportation Security Specialist-Explosives, accelerates the flow of passengers, and increases the safety of DHS personnel and the traveling public.

Our proposed solution is based on Standoff Difference Raman spectroscopy for the analysis of small bulk samples through non-metallic containers and it builds on Pendar X10, a COTS handheld standoff Raman system designed and manufactured by Pendar Technologies. In an initial phase, the key technical challenges related to the analysis of chemicals in containers are de-risked. A new desktop eye-safe instrument concept is then developed, and a prototype is manufactured and tested in a second phase, with automated routines allowing minimally-trained users to successfully operate the system. In a third phase, the algorithms and spectral libraries at the heart of the instrument are improved to address the challenges of chemical identification in a checkpoint environment, where operators face a wide range of stream of commerce items. Finally, a fourth phase addresses network integration of the instrument in the TSA Enterprise Architecture, allowing fleet management, data sharing, and remote reach-back.

The proposed system provides directly actionable information to Transportation Security Officers at the push of a button, while dramatically improving measurement safety. By leveraging the analytical power of Raman spectroscopy, the proposed instrument has the capability to identify thousands of chemicals with proven high selectivity and very low false alarm rate, while the easily upgradable chemical library allows TSA to rapidly react to emerging threats.
topicTitle: Small Bulk Confirmatory Capabilities Through Barriers
awardProposalAbstract:
Explosive threats are a major concern in aviation security and across Homeland Security Enterprise. DHS S&T seeks solutions to enhance current transportation screening capabilities to meet evolving threats, ensuring safety of the traveling public, Transportation Safety Officers, and others within the transportation environment. 

The specific goal of this effort is to develop a technology that can selectively identify explosives through bottles and other containers. The solution proposed allows rapid, safe and confident Alarm Resolution by Transportation Security Officers (TSO) with minimal training, for small bulk threats in containers. It reduces the frequency of Advanced Alarm Resolution, reduces the reliance on Transportation Security Specialist-Explosives, accelerates the flow of passengers, and increases the safety of DHS personnel and the traveling public.

Our proposed solution is based on Standoff Difference Raman spectroscopy for the analysis of small bulk samples through non-metallic containers and it builds on Pendar X10, a COTS handheld standoff Raman system designed and manufactured by Pendar Technologies. In an initial phase, the key technical challenges related to the analysis of chemicals in containers are de-risked. A new desktop eye-safe instrument concept is then developed, and a prototype is manufactured and tested in a second phase, with automated routines allowing minimally-trained users to successfully operate the system. The proposed system provides directly actionable information to Transportation Security Officers at the push of a button, while dramatically improving measurement safety. proposalTitle: Walk-by screening using stationary millimeter-wave sparse linear arrays
proposalAbstract: Cylindrical millimeter-wave (mm-wave) scanners are highly effective for passenger screening at airport checkpoints and are now widely used throughout the world. Millimeter-waves can pass through common clothing material and reflect off the body and concealed threats allowing high-resolution images to be formed that reveal the shape and orientation of concealed items. This technology was developed at the Pacific Northwest National Laboratory (PNNL). While effective, the cylindrical imaging systems do have a number of significant limitations. The passenger is required to stand and pose while the scan is being performed, which could limit throughput and may be inconvenient. Additionally, the large footprint of the scanners may limit their application in some locations. DHS S&T has articulated a checkpoint-of-the-future concept that would minimize passenger delays by utilizing walk-through portals.  PNNL proposes a research and development project to ultimately develop a system that allows passengers to walk through a portal composed of fixed mm-wave linear arrays. This technology would eliminate the cylindrical mechanical scan, which would be replaced through the motion of the passenger. The arrays in this technique would collect data continuously while the motion of the person is determined optically using fixed optical cameras. This would allow convenient, compact imaging systems based on a moderate number of reasonable cost arrays, while preserving the high-resolution possible with linear array-based mm-wave imaging systems.
topicTitle: Checkpoint Passenger Screening

proposalTitle: Linking Flood Risk to Resilience: NFDST Systems Design and Development
proposalAbstract: Resilience is the ability to adapt to changing conditions and withstand and rapidly recover from disruption due to emergencies. To implement resilience, and specifically resilience to floods, communities must understand the types of flooding events to which they are most at risk, the impacts of those events, and, most critical, the types of measures most effective to help them adapt, withstand, and recover from those events. Here, we propose to design and build a comprehensive tool for the National Flood Decision Support Tool (NFDST) to support communities in rapidly assessing their flood risk based on impacts and prioritize resilience investments based on their relative benefit in reducing those impacts. This resilience investment decision support tool will incorporate the most in-depth and current research findings in flood modeling and resilience research, identify and integrate the best available flood risk data and modeling resources into the method, and fully incorporate resilience best practices specifically to meet the needs of community-level implementers of resilience. We will finalize design of a comprehensive systems architecture based on the results of our flood modeling and resilience analysis, design and build a production-ready software application, and deploy the NFDST tool both for use within DHS S&T and within partner communities. Development and deployment will include maintenance and hosting plans to ensure long-term sustainability of the NFDST.
topicTitle: Community Resilience

proposalTitle: Certified DETECT Checkpoint CT Scanner with X-Ray Diffraction
proposalAbstract: CT based detection systems, like the IDSS DETECTTM 1000 (DETECT), have demonstrated significant improvements in Automated Threat Detection (ATD) and image quality for On-Screen Resolution (OSR) of threats such as drugs, explosives, and prohibited items.  DHS is interested in detecting a wider range of threats of small size for both drugs interdiction in postal screening and for explosives detection at the aviation security checkpoint. Such detection requirements pursue improved performance, but at the expense of increased False Alarms (FA). The increased FA has the potential to reduce throughput due to the workload on the operators in both application areas. IDSS is proposing the integration of a Checkpoint CT Scanner with an X-ray Diffraction (XRD) System to directly address these higher detection and FA requirements. This proposed combination, will utilize the XRD system as an effective orthogonal threat resolving technology to further reduce false alarms from the CT (concept shown in Figure 1). The goal of the integration is a real-time solution addressing a wider range of threats while maintaining low FA performance. This integrated solution has applicability to both postal and airport checkpoint screening to achieve operational and performance benefits. The scope of the program is to investigate the feasibility of an integrated system of systems (SoS), combining a certified CT scanner and an XRD, to reduce the overall false-alarm rate, obtain and document the requirements of DHS S&T customer(s), develop a design concept to meet the requirements, develop and deliver the SoS and warranty support for 1 year
topicTitle: Development and Testing of Canine Training Aids

proposalTitle: Integrating the Smart Community to Improve Information Sharing
proposalAbstract:
Technology advances are providing first responders and government officials more tools than ever to achieve situational awareness; operational communications; public alerts and warnings; and command, control, and coordination. However, governance, policy, procedures, and standards initiatives are having a hard time keeping up to support coordinated use of available applications to support information sharing to keep our communities safe. The CA team will engage target audiences from the public and private sectors to capture user experiences and expert recommendations to guide the nation towards integrated technology solutions, enhanced information sharing, and a shared understanding of best practices for preparing the public safety community for the digital future in the following areas with the following benefits:
1. Smart City Performance Indicators Development will inform planning, standards development, and technology integration across public safety and government. 
2. Operational Information Sharing Assessment via the Capability Maturity Model will empower public safety officials to self-assess operational information sharing capabilities and gaps to inform action and strategic planning.
3. Social Media Use Assessment will foster enhanced use of social media within public safety to share real-time, accurate information from public safety to residents (and from residents to public safety) across the nation to support situational awareness and decision making.
4. Wireless Emergency Alerting Standards Assessment will assess and share recommendations for the Common Alerting Protocol to ensure this essential standard can scale, adapt, and perform as requirements evolve from alert originators and receivers.
topicTitle: Community Resilience

proposalTitle: Border Research/Integrattion Transition Environment (BorderRITE)
proposalAbstract: This submission from Salient Federal Solutions, Inc., proposes the Borders & Maritime Research, Integration and Transition Environments (BorderRITE) to support the S&T interests. The objective of BorderRITE is to provide two environments that seamlessly work together to support S&Ts mission. Salient is offering a unique solution to address testing and transition needs for S&T.
topicTitle: Concepts, methodologies, and/or technologies that utilize public as well as private databases

proposalTitle: DHS LRBAA Type III: Hierarchical Software Quality Assurance
proposalAbstract: The advancement of cyber adversaries has led to the increased frequency and complexity of cyberattacks on everything from U.S. military systems to the U.S. voting infrastructure.  By the end of 2021 it is expected that 3.5 million cybersecurity positions will be unfilled and there is a great need to automate cybersecurity as completely as possible.  Hierarchical Software Quality Assurance is a proposed defense mechanism that protects systems along the supply, build and development paths by allowing cyber warriors to deploy quality gates that filter potential threats and by leveraging existing investments in tools and infrastructure.  Herein, we describe an approach that uses hierarchical quality assurance to build operational models (i.e., quality gates) that can be deployed into operational environments.  Specifically, we describe how this novel technology will be used to i) measure source code quality and maturity of Industrial Control Systems (ICS) and Azure cloud environments in supply and production chains, ii) assess the composition, stylometry and origination of software, and iii) identify security zones and sensitive sections of source code.  All software resulting from this effort will be open sourced.
topicTitle: Software Assurance

proposalTitle: Hardware Integrity Verification Utilizing Scanning Electron Microscopy
proposalAbstract: We propose to develop new methodologies to verify the integrity of physical systems, cyber-physical systems (CPS) and Internet of Things (IoT) devices via scanning electron microscopy (SEM), electrical, and software characterization techniques to prevent the unintended manipulation of hardware and software. Vulnerability analysis and novel watermarking/fingerprinting research utilizing scanning electron microscopy are central to the proposed work plan. This conceptual work will then be scaled for use in commercial settings.
topicTitle: Cyber-Physical Systems Security and Security for the Internet of Things Security
companyName: Texas A & M University Central Texas
companyContactTitle: Vice President for Research & Economic Development

proposalTitle: S&T Long Range BAA 14-02 White Paper - DHS Smart Community IoT Interoperable  (SCITI) Sensors and Standards Project
proposalAbstract:
Rapid technology advances in Internet of Things (IoT) technologies, particularly as used in advanced Smart City environments, provide both threats and opportunities for First Responders. This effort seeks to design, develop, prototype, evaluate and put on a path to commercialization two sets of technologies that have the potential for relatively short term capabilities in the field for first responder usage. By building these capabilities to open standards this effort can also influence other industry developments going forward, demonstrating the efficacy of adopting such standards to the standards bodies and commercial equipment providers. 

The two technologies are Unmanned Aerial System (UAS) sensors suitable for indoor use, and fixed inside building sensor suites for deployment on EXIT signs or similar common building features. A key metric of success for this effort is to demonstrate functional prototypes with the potential to be widely commercially available at commercially competitive prices. The first responder use cases for these technologies are focused around search and rescue types of operations in difficult post-incident environments. The commercial applications include new approaches to building security at lower cost for owners. Successful prototypes from this effort may have the opportunity for direct paths to commercial pilot implementations via the new Smart City Works Actuator commercial program.
topicTitle: Smart City Internet of Things (IoT) Interoperable Sensors and Standards

proposalTitle: Analogic Advanced Photon Counting CT
proposalAbstract:
Analogic proposes to develop a prototype Photon Counting Computed Tomography (PCCT) upgrade for the ConneCT carryon baggage screening system. PCCT is a transformative technology that replaces conventional energy integrating CT detectors with semiconductor-based sensors that count the number and energy of each detected X-ray photon. When combined with advanced image reconstruction algorithms, the technology is expected to reduce false alarm rates to < 5% by improving the discrimination of threats from Stream of Commerce (SOC) objects. It should also improve the detection of emerging threats and prohibited items. The bases of these improvements are:
-	precise and quantitative CT density and Zeff images for better material discrimination and object segmentation,
-	sub-millimeter 3D spatial resolution for improved rendering of small objects, wires, electronics, object texture and sharps edges,
-	reduced artifacts from metal and electronics, and
-	the ability to retrospectively reconstruct images of high quality for the application of secondary detection algorithms.
This enhanced performance will drive more efficient security screening and passenger throughput at the checkpoint. Analogic’s goal is to develop an embedded PCCT solution for the ConneCT. The solution, which will be available as an upgrade kit for already fielded systems, will allow the Transportation Security Administration (TSA) to deploy the technology without the additional expense of replacing a previously deployed fleet. Among the aviation security Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs), Analogic is uniquely positioned to develop and bring to market a cost-effective PCCT solution due to decades of design and manufacturing experience and our active research and development programs in medical and aviation security imaging.
topicTitle: Screening at Speed

proposalTitle: The Acceleration of Cyber Technology readiness, Innovation, Commercialization, and Security (TACTICS)
proposalAbstract:
Assured Information Security, Inc. (AIS), along with its subcontractor Robert Rodriguez and Associates, LLC, proposes The Acceleration of Cyber Technology readiness, Innovation, Commercialization, and Security (TACTICS), a five year, 5.2 million effort designed to advance the functionality, security, and robustness of technologies developed under DHS S&T Cyber Security Division (CSD) funding. TACTICS will accelerate technology readiness and create opportunities that enhance viability in the commercial marketplace.  

TACTICS will accomplish these objectives through three tasks; 1) the acquisition, assessment, and red-teaming of cybersecurity technologies, 2) the piloting, experimentation, and deployment of technologies into representative environments or among early adopters or potential end-users, and 3) collaboration workshops, forums, and events that facilitate cyber entrepreneurship, awareness, innovation, investment, and public/private cooperation.

DHS S&T CSD has achieved a high degree of success in efforts to forage, test, pilot, and transition cutting-edge solutions to a diverse set of end-users with disparate missions. Existing testing efforts, being largely functional in nature, have ensured that capabilities align with end-user needs, while experiments and pilots have been an effective mechanism for identifying and reducing the risks surrounding the deployment of novel solutions. The TACTICS efforts innovative approach to enhancing CSDs testing, transition, and commercialization practices as well as documentation, metrics and reporting will serve as a logical extension to current practice, and will have an immediate and profound impact on the overall technology transition and commercialization capabilities of CSD as well as stakeholders across the Homeland Security Enterprise.
topicTitle: Experiments and Pilots

proposalTitle: Building Resilience Through a Community Lifeline System
proposalAbstract: The purpose of this project is to increase and enhance the use of the Federal Emergency Management Agency’s (FEMA) Community Lifeline construct by Federal, State and Local emergency management agencies.  The G&H Team is proposing a highly collaborative stakeholder engagement and technology development project to create a Community Lifeline System (CLS) that addresses the current patchwork and labor-intensive approach to Lifeline reporting which hampers the ability of FEMA and State and Local agencies to make critical resource decisions during and after incidents.  The CLS will be designed and fielded to simplify, standardize and, to the extent possible, automate the identification, acquisition, management, organization, analysis, and communications of Lifeline status reports with accompanying data to support informed and timely data-driven decisions through all phases of the emergency management lifecycle.  The CLS will be created through the integration and development of planning and data collection tools, templates, architecture integration strategies and information sharing capabilities to support the processes in FEMA’s Lifeline Toolkit, Incident Stabilization guide and other plans and guidance documents.  The use of technology platforms and systems commonly used by FEMA and State and Local emergency management agencies and working with the Central U.S. Earthquake Consortium’s (CUSEC) newly established Consortium for Emergency Management Technology (CEST), will improve the implementation of the CLS for use by the emergency management community.  The system development lifecycle will include agile and iterative development, along with testing and evaluation with stakeholders and a transition of the CLS to operational entities over a 3-year period.
topicTitle: Technology Acceptance (ESB)

proposalTitle: DECIDE Energy
proposalAbstract: NUARI's Distributed Environment for Critical Infrastructure Decision-making Exercises (DECIDE) platform was developed to engage the financial sector leadership and business process owners in situational awareness of cybersecurity impacts and effects at the sector and business operations level. DECIDE creates a focused and collaborative environment to explore internal, firm-to-firm, and sector understanding of the systems, processes, regulatory, and legal effects of cybersecurity events. DECIDE allows a sector and its dependencies to proactively identify, analyze, assess, and coordinate activities to mitigate critical infrastructure and systemic risk. DECIDE and associated financial sector exercises have been critical drivers for the development of a Financial Sector Playbook and greater situational awareness across the sector of cybersecurity impacts on tactics, techniques, and procedures. Use of the DECIDE Platform strengthens the organizational capability to respond to and recover from cybersecurity events, as well as the ability to execute these plans under conditions of operational duress. In highly interconnected infrastructures, response across the sector is essential for survivability. DECIDE platform allows for the maturity of both the individual and the sector. NUARI is proposing the development of an energy-sector focused DECIDE platform, which includes engagement with firms, industry organizations, and information-sharing entities. This proposed project is consistent with CSD.21.
topicTitle: Cyber Situational Understanding

proposalTitle: SeaSCRAPER: Seabottom Surveillance Cable for Real-time Awareness of Point-of-Entry Risks
proposalAbstract:
Maritime domain awareness and surveillance of US ports and coastlines is a critical challenge for the Department of Homeland Security (DHS). Current technologies for maritime border security include hydrophone, video/thermal, and radar sensors. However, none of these approaches provide a continuous vessel detection, classification, and tracking capability spanning hundreds of kilometers at low cost, high accuracy, and with minimal infrastructure. To address the need for a scalable vessel detection, classification, and localization capability, Raytheon BBN Technologies (BBN) proposes the Sea bottom Surveillance Cable for Real-time Awareness of Point-of-Entry Risks (SeaSCRAPER) system to provide the densest possible sensor coverage at lowest cost per linear kilometer from coastline to deep water. A single system will provide real-time underwater surveillance and vessel classification for 50 to 100 km of border. The enabling technologies are 1) Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS), which exploits the strain on a fiber optic cable caused by acoustic disturbances in the environment via optical interrogation of the fiber, and 2) Machine Learning algorithms for accurate and automated detection and classification processing. 

The project base effort will validate the feasibility of the system by experimentally demonstrating and assessing its performance using commercial-off-the-shelf (COTS) hardware and software. Proposed options will 1) advance the technology through the development and demonstration of a fully functional experimental prototype in near shore (continental shelf) waters, and 2) optimize the SeaSCRAPER system for improved performance in deep (multi-kilometer) water far offshore.
topicTitle: Maritime Domain Awareness Technologies

proposalTitle: Interrogation with Neutrons - Screening for Threats and Interdicting Narcotics in Cargo at High Throughput (IN-STINCT)
proposalAbstract: As DHS Customs and Border Protection (CBP) increases the percentage of commercial cargo and vehicles subject to primary inspection at major Ports of Entry (POEs), orthogonal Non-Intrusive Inspection (NII) technologies at secondary screening can reduce the dependence on touch labor, increase the overall screening throughput of commerce, and increase screening effectiveness and the rate of contraband seizures. Phoenix proposes a 3-year development program resulting in an advanced prototype high intensity neutron-based screening system at Technology Readiness Level (TRL) 7 to augment existing CBP screening technology at major POEs and provide additional information about complex cargo loads. In the early stages of the project, Phoenix will explore leveraging its existing high TRL systems in both neutron-induced activation and fast neutron radiography that have been previously fielded in operations for industrial use cases to provide DHS with a trade space that balances performance with size, weight, power and cost (SWAP-C). At the conclusion of this project, the advanced, safe NII prototype developed for the government will be ready for field testing in an operational environment and subsequent commercialization.
topicTitle: Non-Intrusive Screening to Detect Synthetic Opioids and Other Illicit Drugs

proposalTitle: Center for Innovative Technology - Commercial First Innovation
proposalAbstract:
Center for Innovative Technology (CIT) proposes to deliver a novel set of prototypes incorporating new applications of technologies to demonstrate near-term capabilities responsive to current operational challenges of DHS components. We will deliver Unique Prototype Technologies (identified as a Type II research area in the LRBAA) including proof-of-concept prototypes that can be demonstrated in an operational environment. Associated Type I research will validate the efficacy of this process and outcomes via scientific study and experimentation.

The CIT team uses a replicable prototyping process called Commercial First Innovation (Commercial First), which we will initially apply under topic SEC CYB 09-03 and MGMT 08-01 but is readily extensible to produce operationally-ready prototypes in other topic areas such as SEC CYB 09-02: IoT Security, MGMT 02-04: Hybrid Technologies for CPB, and others. The Commercial First process has proven the ability to deliver transition-ready prototypes in areas of DHS component needs by incorporating techniques from commercial innovation and investment communities; the result is higher transition success rate to operational use for our capabilities.
topicTitle: Smart Cities

proposalTitle: Detection Canine Sciences, Innovation, Technology, and Education (DCSITE)
proposalAbstract: The Detection Canine Sciences, Innovation, Technology, and Education (DCSITE) program will catalyze transformational advances in detection canine sciences with broad application to the whole of the homeland security enterprise. The scope of DCSITE will be transdisciplinary and  comprehensive of all threats, integrating best scientific practices in behavior and cognition, olfactory neuroscience, analytical chemistry, genomics, reproduction, performance, well-being, metrology and enginerring. DCSITE will be anchored by the Auburn University College of Veterinary Medicine, Canine Performance Sciences program, and leverage the entirety of Auburn University resources and external partnerships as necessary to address both canine-centric and essential, canine-agnostic threat detection challenges. Nimble, vertically integrated operations will be assured through a whole of Auburn University approach. An advisory board will be established to guide and assess DCSITE program performance. Program objectives will be intelligence driven in concert with end-user mission priorities to address current gaps, needs and emerging threats.Education, including regional/national outreach and engagement of historically underrepresented groups, will be fostered by the DCSITE program to build program capacity. DCSITE activities will be organized across interrelated  domains within the three focal areas including the working dog, operational dynamics, and targets of detection inclusively responsive to current DHSST Canine topic areas in "R&D Canine Structure and Function", "Operational Test and Evaluation of Technologies" and "Development and Testing of Training Aids". DCSITE will be a nexus for capability building, operations enhancement, and innovation in threat detection and domestic canine development with broad interagency, regional, and internatinal impact.

DCSITE will be a nexus for capability building, operations enhancement, and innovation in threat detection and domestic canine development with broad interagency, regional, and internatinal impact.

proposalTitle: Soter
proposalAbstract: Syzygy Integration LLC ('Syzygy') is pleased to propose the Soter program to DHS S&T for the MGMT02-05 topics area.  Soter was the Greek god of safety (not an acronym for the proposal), and Syzygy feels strongly that the proposed system will help increase the safety of the United States with an enterprise situational awareness platform across DHS and state/county/city responders.  Soter will scale the Team Awareness Kit (TAK) ecosystem to have native integration with Low-Cost Tactical Communications, handle 100's of thousands of concurrent clients, provide a way for law enforcement to bring your own device (BYOD) and have the partition for DHS information instantly accredited to share operational data for all DHS operational units rapidly.  Syzygy's end-state goal is to have the Soter TAK implementation be available for nationwide fielding at the end of this effort to have all federal, state, county, city, and local law enforcement/responders into a single accredited situational awareness network at a very cheap cost to build and sustain.  The development process that Syzygy will use on the Soter program is Continuous Test and Evaluation to reduce risk, mitigate issues, and result in reduced system sustainment costs for transition.

proposalTitle: Emergency Response Information Fusion & Innovative Analytics Hub
proposalAbstract:
The current federal fire incident reporting and management system falls short of the real-time, actionable intelligence regarding local emergency incidents as well as insights from regional and national data aggregation that the United States’ fire and emergency medical services (EMS) desire. Although the National Fire Incident Reporting System (NFIRS) currently processes approximately 20-25 million combined fire and EMS records each year, that data is shared publicly nearly 2 years or more after the incidents occurred. A 2-year lag limits the value of data set because the hazards fire and EMS departments respond to across the United States are evolving at a faster pace. As a result, departments generally rely on only their locally collected data for decision making. For large, urban centers there may be sufficient data and internal resources to analyze the data for trends to inform the departments, however that is not always the case. Departments with lower-socioeconomic status, despite potentially benefitting more from data-driven intelligence, often lack the resources needed to aggregate and analyze data themselves.
To address these gaps, the proposed platform will be built using existing interoperable data exchange standards and on an open solutions cloud-based architecture that will be designed to ensure scalability and flexibility in integrating data. It will also be designed to ensure adaptability to the evolving needs of local emergency responders and achieve greater equity of service availability at the local level by offering no-cost direct contribution capabilities as well as through credentialed third-party vendors.
The architecture of the proposed data system is based on a data mesh strategy. In a data mesh architecture, the focus is on decentralized ownership and governance. Some of the advantages of this decentralized approach include improved scalability (inherently designed to scale horizontally making it easier to add departments and users), improved data governance (data security and privacy policy framework can be established at the local levels), and improved performance (decentralized components can be deployed and tested independently and can reduce the read/write rates when entries are produced by those closest to the data).
Each department (node within the data mesh) will feature an analysis engine to provide actionable data back to the end users in a web-based analytics hub. This forward-facing component will provide end users with interactive access to their core data, analytics on their data (e.g., statistical performance across the department, performance against standards), and predictive measures all enhanced through the combination of secondary and tertiary data streams.
By enabling each node to directly process data and share data through linked nodes established by local governance rules (mutual/automatic aid agreements, state level data, federal level data), this platform can provide a near real-time assessment of the severity of the U.S. fire problem. Organizations will be empowered with the data and insights needed to better deploy resources, adapt to changing hazards, and develop targeted public education campaigns. Codes and standards-making bodies will be able to source and utilize data that is relevant at the time, and not months or years later. Ultimately, state-of-the-art analytics will support the mission to increase the safety of first responders and the civilians they protect. These analytics will better enable departments to make informed decisions in near-real time.

proposalTitle: DEBIASE – Development and Engineering of Biometric and Identity Accuracy, Scalability and Equitability
proposalAbstract:
DHS components are increasing adoption of biometric technologies, notably non-contact biometric modalities including face recognition. In the air travel domain, CBP has deployed the Traveler Verification Service (TVS) to automate identity processing for international departures and arrivals and TSA has implemented the CAT-C system to verify traveler identity. Increased adoption of face recognition and other non-contact biometrics at DHS is likely to continue over the next five years with an expansion to new use-cases including: identification and verification at land and seaports of entry (POEs), remote identity verification for access to government services, and paperless identity experience at TSA checkpoints. Public deployment of these technologies has led to new challenges. First, it has raised questions of technology accessibility, equitability, and fairness with respect to protected demographic groups (e.g., race, color, gender, age, and disability status). Second, the ever-expanding DHS use-cases for biometrics continue to challenge the limits of commercial biometric systems, which can vary drastically in performance and applicability. Third, the same deep convolutional neural nets (DCNNs) that underlie today’s cutting-edge biometric systems have enabled new ways in which these systems may be subverted. Finally, as DHS identity processes are increasingly automated, CBP and TSA officers must be able to team with the technology such that the total system performance is enhanced. 
SAIC’s Identity and Data Science Laboratory (IDSL) is proposing to perform applied research to address these new and continuing challenges as follows:
1.	Perform comprehensive research, analysis and reporting to enable DHS S&T to address questions regarding the efficiency, effectiveness, public acceptance, equitability and privacy implications of current commercial biometric technologies by performing technology experimentation and data gathering activities, in dedicated laboratory spaces, at geographically dispersed locations, operational locations, and using online platforms; and by performing customized data analyses and automating common analysis tasks. 
2.	Inform procurement activities to increase the performance of biometric systems by providing DHS S&T with up-to-date information on the capabilities and performance of commercial technologies and communicate technology gaps to industry and academia for mitigation. 
3.	Increase automation in routine DHS identity tasks by experimenting with strategies for teaming humans and algorithms in DHS workflows to identify optimal methods.
4.	Inform biometric technology standards across the homeland security enterprise and internationally by translating results of applied technology research into standardizable metrics and procedures, incorporating these into ISO and similar standards, and assisting DHS components in incorporating standard metrics in assessments of existing biometric technology systems and technology requirements for future systems.

proposalTitle: A Non-Traditional Approach to Support the Cyber Apex Program
proposalAbstract: Cyber Apex Solutions (CAS), llc, a non-traditional government contractor, was established to fill the gap in the market related to an entity that could provide the proper support to DHS S&T Cyber Security Division's (CSD) Apex Program (i.e., CSD.19) and related technology development, testing, and deployment efforts.  CAS's mission is to execute R&D and prototyping/piloting initiatives using an innovative procurement means and a unique consortium made up of a number of non-traditional government contractors.  CAS has proposed a means that give it and CSD the flexibility to leverage both the Federal government's and CAS's consortium members' technology, people, and processes to gather and understand the financial sector's security requirements and user needs and close the sector's capability gaps effectively and efficiently. In addition, and most importantly, this initiative will result in viable, new commercially available technologies and solutions that will help secure the financial sector and possibly others. CAS is proposing a five-year award that mirrors the initial five-year time line of the Cyber Security Apex Program.
topicTitle: Next Generation Cyber Infrastructure

proposalTitle: Artificial Intelligence Enabled Passive TeraHertz Screening for Limited Mobility Passengers
proposalAbstract: Modern On-Person Screening technology has enabled higher levels of security and more flexible screening of persons in Airport Checkpoint environments. While these advances have enabled higher throughput and security, they underlying technology assumes a level of mobility not applicable to all passengers. It is proposed that using data-driven intelligent algorithms, cutting edge On-Person Screening technology ready for deployment can also be enabled to robustly screen passengers of limited mobility in a gender agnostic fashion without requiring divestiture of wheelchairs or other mobility aids.The Thruvision CPC16 is an On Person Screening system based on Passive TeraHertz technology that can detect non divested items at a distance of up to 12 ft. Analytical AI has developed the. artificial intelligence algorithms for this system for full mobility passenger and aim as part of this proposal to extend that capability as a proof of concept to limited mobility passengers.
topicTitle: Streamlined Airport Checkpoint Screening for Limited Mobility Passengers
solicitationTitle: DEPARTMENT OF HOMELAND SECURITY (DHS) SMALL BUSINESS INNOVATION RESEARCH (SBIR) PROGRAM  FY 22

proposalTitle: Handheld Immobile Traveler Screening Tool
proposalAbstract:
Check point screening of passengers is key to mitigating threats to aviation. This essential mission must be performed while expediting traffic flow and without discrimination to elderly or disabled travelers.  The screening mission is challenged by the inaccessibility of Advanced Imaging Technology (AIT) and metal detector walk-through portals to travelers with mobility disabilities and certain medical conditions.  The result of these challenges is that passengers with limited or no ability to walk are relegated to a more time-consuming and less efficient screening process.  

To fill this gap, Spectral Labs proposes to develop and test a compact, hand-held device based on millimeter wave (MMW) imaging which can be used to detect contraband and threats such as weapons and explosives hidden on the passenger who may not be able to safely be separated from mobility devices, such as wheelchairs and walkers, or external medical devices, such as oxygen generators and insulin pumps.  By allowing for rapid, non-contact screening of the passenger, this benefits the privacy traveler, the efficacy of the Transportation Security Officer, and the overall safety of air travel.

In addition to the specific role for limited mobility travelers at TSA checkpoints, the device will benefit multiple missions at the government customers at the Federal, State, and Local levels, from supplementing fixed AIT installations, resolving alarms without the need for a pat-down, to primary screening for pop-up itinerant screening such as special events, and locations which cannot accommodate the cost or infrastructure for a full AIT installation.
topicTitle: Streamlined Airport Checkpoint Screening for Limited Mobility Passengers
